examples.title: One-Time Pad Encryption/Decryption
examples.description: Implementation of OTP encryption and decryption using XOR operation on binary data.
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the core OTP principle: encryption and decryption use the same XOR operation because XOR is self-inverse (A⊕A=0 and A⊕0=A). The key requirement checks ensure the fundamental OTP condition is met.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the length of the plaintext

examples.title: OTP Key Generation with True Randomness
examples.description: Implementation of secure key generation for OTP using true random number generator.
examples.explanation: This code emphasizes the critical requirement of true randomness. Hardware TRNG sources include thermal noise, radioisotope decay, or quantum effects. Using deterministic PRNGs breaks perfect secrecy because an attacker who knows the seed can regenerate the key.

examples.title: Two-Time Pad Attack Detection and Analysis
examples.description: Demonstration of how to detect key reuse and the attack methodology when reuse occurs.
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates the devastating effect of key reuse. When the same key encrypts two messages, XORing the ciphertexts eliminates the key completely. The attacker then uses linguistic patterns ('cribs') to recover plaintexts, a technique famously used to decrypt Soviet spy messages.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n×m) where n=ciphertext length, m=crib length (crib-dragging)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for storing intermediate XOR results
